perturbation.original_text: 5.3.1 Subject to Articles 5.1 and 5.2, the Parties acknowledge and agree that CERES shall have the exclusive right, with the right to grant sublicenses to this right, to use and commercially exploit JOINT INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY (including but not limited to RELEASED VARIETIES) and OTHER RESEARCH RESULTS for any and all uses and fields, including, but not limited to, use in the COLLABORATION CROPS and crops other than the COLLABORATION CROPS. Any commercialization (including, but not limited to, the grant of commercialization licenses) of JOINT INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY and/or OTHER RESEARCH RESULTS shall result in CERES paying IGER a reasonable remuneration (whether as a royalty or in some other form as the Parties may agree) to be negotiated in good faith. The terms for remuneration will be specified, with respect to specific RESEARCH PROJECTS, JOINT INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY and/or OTHER RESEARCH RESULTS, either in the respective SCHEDULES or in other agreements to be executed by the Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.3.1 Subject to Articles 5.1 and 5.2, the Parties acknowledge and agree that CERES shall have the exclusive right, with the right to grant sublicenses to this right, to use and commercially exploit JOINT INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY (including but not limited to RELEASED VARIETIES) and OTHER RESEARCH RESULTS for any and all uses and fields, including, but not limited to, use in the COLLABORATION CROPS and crops other than the COLLABORATION CROPS. Any commercialization (including, but not limited to, the grant of commercialization licenses) of JOINT INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY and/or OTHER RESEARCH RESULTS shall result in CERES paying IGER remuneration (whether as a royalty or in some other form as the Parties may agree).
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'to be negotiated in good faith. The terms for remuneration will be specified, with respect to specific RESEARCH PROJECTS, JOINT INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY and/or OTHER RESEARCH RESULTS, either in the respective SCHEDULES or in other agreements to be executed by the Parties.' an in-text contradiction is introduced. While the text specifies that CERES will pay IGER remuneration, it fails to establish a process or location on where the specific payments are to be negotiated or specified. This introduces ambiguity because without a negotiation process or specified terms, the obligation becomes unenforceable, this contradicts the intentions in articles 5.1 and 5.2.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.3.1 Subject to Articles 5.1 and 5.2, the Parties acknowledge and agree that CERES shall have the exclusive right, with the right to grant sublicenses to this right, to use and commercially exploit JOINT INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY (including but not limited to RELEASED VARIETIES) and OTHER RESEARCH RESULTS for any and all uses and fields, including, but not limited to, use in the COLLABORATION CROPS and crops other than the COLLABORATION CROPS. Any commercialization (including, but not limited to, the grant of commercialization licenses) of JOINT INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY and/or OTHER RESEARCH RESULTS shall result in CERES paying IGER remuneration (whether as a royalty or in some other form as the Parties may agree).
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the clause specifying negotiation in good faith and the process for specifying remuneration terms in schedules or agreements. This omission creates a contradiction because, while CERES is obligated to pay IGER remuneration, the lack of a defined negotiation process and specification mechanism makes the obligation unenforceable, which contradicts the original intention to establish a clear payment structure as implied by articles 5.1 and 5.2. The absence of these details introduces ambiguity and weakens the enforceability of the payment obligation, creating a direct conflict with the original intent.

perturbation.original_text: 4.8 The Parties shall consult as soon as possible but in any case within twenty (20) days of receiving an INVENTION NOTICE whether to proceed to obtain intellectual property protection on the disclosed JOINT INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY or whether to protect the same through other methods. CERES shall have the first option to pursue such protection in its best judgment in the joint names of both Parties. If CERES so elects by written notice within sixty (60) days of such initial consultation, CERES shall be the "ADMINISTERING PARTY" for the purposes of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.8 The Parties shall consult as soon as possible but in any case within twenty (20) days of receiving an INVENTION NOTICE whether to proceed to obtain intellectual property protection on the disclosed JOINT INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY or whether to protect the same through other methods. CERES shall have the first option to pursue such protection in its best judgment in the joint names of both Parties.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting 'If CERES so elects by written notice within sixty (60) days of such initial consultation, CERES shall be the "ADMINISTERING PARTY" for the purposes of this Agreement.' it creates an in-text contradiction, it creates uncertainty in who is actually responsible for the the process of securing intellectual property, this contradicts 4.8.1 which speaks of the ADMINISTERING PARTY and how they are responsible for retaining counsel.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.8.1 The ADMINISTERING PARTY shall be responsible for retaining counsel, overseeing the process of securing intellectual property protection (i.e., the preparation, filing and prosecution of patent or plant variety rights application(s)) and maintaining intellectual property protection for the mutual benefit of the Parties, in its best judgment, for that which it has prosecution responsibility. In addition to other reporting responsibilities provided below, the ADMINISTERING PARTY shall promptly notify the other Party following retention of counsel. The Parties will provide, and cause their respective employees, agents and students to provide, all reasonable assistance which may be required in connection with the filing and prosecution of such intellectual property rights, including without limitation the signing of documents.
perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause defining how CERES becomes the 'ADMINISTERING PARTY' directly contradicts section 4.8.1. Without this definition, the contract lacks clarity on who is responsible for retaining counsel and overseeing intellectual property protection, rendering 4.8.1 unenforceable. The original text provides a clear process for designating the 'ADMINISTERING PARTY', while the changed text creates ambiguity, directly impacting the obligations outlined in 4.8.1. This directly conflicts with the 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: 14.3.1 In case of termination on the basis of Article 14.1 (b) if CERES is the breaching Party, Article 14.1 (c) if CERES ceases substantially all activities in the COLLABORATION CROPS, Article 14.1 (d) (i), (ii) or (iii) or Article 14.1. (e) (iii), at or about the effective date of termination, the Parties will negotiate in good faith to reach agreement as to the rights to use and commercially exploit JOINT INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY not covered by any relevant license/commercialization agreement between the Parties, which rights will be addressed in one or more written agreements. If the Parties fail to reach agreement within ninety (90) days after the start of such negotiations, which shall be evidenced by written notice from one Party to the other initiating such negotiations, each Party shall have the non-exclusive right to use and commercially exploit JOINT INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY for any and all purposes, with the right to grant sublicenses, subject to the obligations of the first sentence of Article 14.3, provided that no licenses on any transgenes or transgenic technologies of the other Party shall be included or implied.
perturbation.changed_text: 14.3.1 In case of termination on the basis of Article 14.1 (b) if CERES is the breaching Party, Article 14.1 (c) if CERES ceases substantially all activities in the COLLABORATION CROPS, Article 14.1 (d) (i), (ii) or (iii) or Article 14.1. (e) (iii), at or about the effective date of termination, the Parties will negotiate in good faith to reach agreement as to the rights to use and commercially exploit JOINT INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY not covered by any relevant license/commercialization agreement between the Parties, which rights will be addressed in one or more written agreements. If the Parties fail to reach agreement within ninety (90) days after the start of such negotiations, each Party shall have the non-exclusive right to use and commercially exploit JOINT INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting ', with the right to grant sublicenses, subject to the obligations of the first sentence of Article 14.3, provided that no licenses on any transgenes or transgenic technologies of the other Party shall be included or implied.' It creates an in-text contradiction by removing a crucial part of the clause that sets a clear right on using the joint intellectual property after the termination of the contract. Now, with this clause missing, it is ambigious whether each party has the right to grant sublicenses subject to the conditions that must be fulfilled, that can have serious consequences in determining who can use and to whom the joint intellectual property can be offered to.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 14.3.1 In case of termination on the basis of Article 14.1 (b) if CERES is the breaching Party, Article 14.1 (c) if CERES ceases substantially all activities in the COLLABORATION CROPS, Article 14.1 (d) (i), (ii) or (iii) or Article 14.1. (e) (iii), at or about the effective date of termination, the Parties will negotiate in good faith to reach agreement as to the rights to use and commercially exploit JOINT INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY not covered by any relevant license/commercialization agreement between the Parties, which rights will be addressed in one or more written agreements. If the Parties fail to reach agreement within ninety (90) days after the start of such negotiations, each Party shall have the non-exclusive right to use and commercially exploit JOINT INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY.
perturbation.justification: The omission of the phrase ', with the right to grant sublicenses, subject to the obligations of the first sentence of Article 14.3, provided that no licenses on any transgenes or transgenic technologies of the other Party shall be included or implied' directly contradicts the original intention and creates ambiguity regarding the specific rights and limitations associated with the use of Joint Intellectual Property after termination. The contradicted text is the same clause in its original form, and now the absence of the omitted phrase introduces uncertainty about the ability to grant sublicenses and the restrictions on licensing transgenes or transgenic technologies.
